title: Improved method for welding and metal powder therefor
abstract: A metal powder mixture for welding joints (e.g. by flame spraying) consists of nickel powder having a particle size of less than 150 mesh (U.S.) in an amount of 10-50% the balance being a nickel base alloy powder. Suitable alloy powders disclosed are:- .Ni.. .Cr.5-15%.- .Si.2-6%.1-6% .B.1-4%.1-4% .Fe.0.1-8%.0.2-5% .C.0.1-1.5%.0.01-0.3%

A 279, A 349, A 51 X, A 541, A 615, A 681, A 699, Improved method for Welding and Metal Powder therefor We, EUTECTIC WELDING ALLOYS CORPORATION, a corporation organized and existing under the laws of the State of New York, United States of America, of 40-40 172nd Street, Flushing 58, State of New York, United States of America, do hereby declare the invention, for which we pray that a patent may be granted to us, and the method by which it is to be performed, to be particularly described in and by the following statement:-
This invention relates to a method for welding joints and more particularly to such a method for flame spraying joints.
Although conventional metal powders can be satisfactorily deposited on, for example, joints at acceptable temperatures, these deposits are not as ductile as desired.
An object of this invention is to provide a method for welding in which the metal powder possess advantageous characteristics superior to conventional materials with improved ductility also.
According to the invention there is provided a process for flame spraying a nickel deposit comprising the steps of adding nickel powder to a nickel base alloy to provide a heterogeneous mixture, said nickel powder having a particle size of less than 150 mesh and being in the range of 10 to 501 % by weight of said mixture, and simultaneously flame spraying and fusing said mixture at a temperature above the melting point of the alloy and below the melting point of the nickel powder to obtain a homogeneous deposit.
Throughout this specification the mesh sizes given are in the U S Standard Fine Sieve Series.
Surprisingly, the melting temperature of the mixture is substantially that of the nickel base alloy although the nickel powder itself has a higher melting temperature and comprises such a relatively large amount of the -mixture or filler metal However, the resultant deposited metal has vastly improved ductility over the nickel base alloy alone The superior qualities of the pure nickel are thereby obtained in the deposit at lower temperatures because of the interreaction between the pure nickel and the alloy In an advantageous form of this invention the nickel powder comprises from 30 to 40 % of the mixture.
The invention will now be described by way of example with reference to the accompanying drawings, in which:
Figure 1 shows the microstructure of a conventional nickel base alloy; Figure 2 shows the microstructure of the nickel base alloy shown in Figure 1 after deposit in accordance with this invention with nickel powder; Figure 3 shows the microstructure of another nickel base alloy; Figure 4 shows the microstructure of the m "I X G 6 c rm; 1,108,813 nickel base alloy shown in Figure 3 after deposit in accordance with this invention with nickel powder; Figure 5 shows the microstructure of still another nickel base alloy; and Figure 6 shows the microstructure of the nickel base alloy shown in Figure 5 after deposit in accordance with this invention with nickel powder.
In Figure 1 is shown the microstructure of a nickel base alloy of the following composition: 2 6 % Si, 1 5 % B, 0 5 '% Fe, 0 031 % Q, balance Ni Figure 2 shows the same alloy after a 30 % addition of nickel powder deposited in accordance with this invention As shown in Figure 1, the grains 10 of nickel are relatively small as compared with the much larger grains 12 shown in Figure 2 The proportion of the hard inter-granular constituents in Figure 2 are reduced to a level which permits the alloy to possess greater ductility and lower indentation hardness as a result of the % addition of pure nickel powder.
Similarly, Figures 3 and 4 show the effect of a 40 % addition of nickel powder to Ni B-4 nickel base alloy of the following composition: 3 54 %'/, Si, 2 02 % B, 0 45 %, Fe, 0 04 % C, balance Ni As shown in Figure 4 the resultant nickel grains 14 of the nickel solid solution after deposit in accordance with this invention are exceedingly larger than the nickel grains 16 shown in Figure 3 The microstructure shown in Figure 4 indicates the greatly reduced amount of hard interangular constituents which thereby results in vastly improved ductility.
Figures 5 and 6 show the effect of a 30 % nickel powder addition to Ni B-1, nickel base alloy of the following composition: 14 9 % Cr, 4 3 % Si, 3 4 %B, 4 2 %/o Fe, 0 68 % C, balance Ni As compared with the very hard deposit having small nickel grains 18 shown in Figure 5 the large nickel rich grains 20 shown in Figure 6 of a deposit in accordance with this invention contain boron, silicon, and iron in a solid solution and indicate the improved ductility and lower indentation Mixture hardness of the heterogeneous mixture.
This improved ductility is obtained with the retention of the desirable qualities, such as the ability of being deposited at satisfactory melting temperatures, of conventional nickel base alloys by adding from 10 to 50 %l/ by weight of nickel powder to the nickel base alloys Some of the advantageous nickel base alloys are described as Ni B-1, Ni B-2, Ni B-3 and Ni B-4 in Table I, page 4 of the American Welding Society Specification
A 5.8-62 T The resultant nickel mixture is particularly effective when it is sprayed and fused in a single operation on, for example, a joint to obtain a low cost homogeneous nickel deposit having a high remelt temperature than the temperature at which the heterogeneous powder mixture is applied Surprisingly, the nickel mixture has, upon spraying, excellent wetting-on flow characteristics This result is particularly unexpected when the much high melting point of the powder is considered For example, the melting point of the pure nickel is 26520 F as compared with an alloy melting point of 24000 F However, where the nickel powder is of particle size finer than 150 mesh, the powder is very rapidly taken into solution at the temperature normally used to deposit the nickel base alloy itself A fluid molten pool is thereby produced which wets the base metal and can be manipulated very similarly to the nickel base alloy involved That the molten pool dissolves the nickel completely is proven by the fact that no segregation of nickel has been found in the microstructures of numerous deposits made with up to 50 % nickel powder addition.
Figures 2, 4 and 6, for example, show that the microstructure produced by nickel powder additions is characterized by greater solution of the hard constituents.
The following table shows the effect of nickel powder additions on lowering the hardness of the nickel base alloy powder of the following composition: 2 6 % Si, 1 51 % B, % Fe, 0 03 i X C, balance Ni.
Wt %h Nickel Powder Rockwell B Hardness 1 0 RB 93-98 2 10 RB 90-91 3 15 RB 86-89 4 20 RB 78-82 30 RB 75-78 6 40 RB 65-70 7 50 RB 57-62 1,-108,813 As is readily apparent from the preceding table, substantial reductions of deposit hardness are obtained with the use of the heterogeneous powders, especially where the pow ders constitute at least 10 % by weight of the mixture Further, the consistency of hardness values along the length of a given deposit clearly indicates that the powdered nickel has gone completely into solution.

claims:
WHAT WE CLAIM IS:-
1 A process for flame spraying a nickel deposit comprising the steps of adding nickel powder to a nickel base alloy to form a heterogeneous mixture, said nickel powder having a particle size of less than 150 mesh and being present in the range of 10 to 5 WI/, by weight of said mixture, and simultaneously flame spraying and fusing said mixture at a temperature above the melting point of said alloy and below the melting point of said nickel powder to obtain a homogeneous deposit.
2 A process as claimed in claim 1, wherein said nickel powders are in the range of 30 to 401 % by weight of said mixture.
3 A process as claimed in claim 1 or claim 2, wherein said nickel base alloy consists of the following constituents in the indicated ranges of percentages by weight:
